perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Proprietary Information will require that all third parties to this Agreement, if any, to which it may give such proprietary information protect the same in accordance with the provisions contained herein. The third party shall be required to execute an agreement with the same provisions as contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Proprietary Information will encourage third parties to which it may give such proprietary information to consider protecting the same in a manner that is generally consistent with the provisions contained herein. The third party might consider executing an agreement with provisions similar to those contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will require" to "will encourage to consider" and "shall be required to execute" to "might consider executing" significantly weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information, making it discretionary rather than mandatory. This ambiguity makes enforcement problematic.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alabama Trade Secrets Act (Ala. Code § 8-27-3) allows for injunctive relief against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the requirement for third parties to protect proprietary information, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation and obtain injunctive relief, as the original contract provides a much clearer expectation of confidentiality that aligns with the protection intended by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022) - Misappropriation. :: 2022 Code of Alabama :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Code of Alabama Title 8 - Commercial Law and Consumer Protection. Chapter 27 - Alabama Trade Secrets Act. Section 8-27-3 - Misappropriation. Universal Citation: AL Code § 8-27-3 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Section 8-27-3 Misappropriation. A person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other for misappropriation of the trade secret if: (1) That person discovered the trade secret by improper means; (2) That person's disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in that person by the other; (3) That person learned the trade secret from a third person, and knew or should have known that (i) the information was a trade secret and (ii) that the trade secret had been appropriated under circumstances which violate the provisions of (1) or (2), above; or (4) That person learned the information and knew or should have known that it was a trade secret and that its disclosure was made to that person by mistake. (Acts 1987, No. 87-669, p. 1195, §2.) Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Alabama may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information linked to on the state site. perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the protection of proprietary information by changing mandatory language to discretionary language. The original text states that each party 'will require' third parties to protect proprietary information and that the third party 'shall be required to execute' an agreement. The changed text, however, states that each party 'will encourage' third parties to 'consider protecting' the information and that the third party 'might consider executing' an agreement. This weakening could hinder the ability to prove misappropriation and obtain injunctive relief under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act (Ala. Code § 8-27-3), as the original contract provides a clearer expectation of confidentiality. Specifically, Ala. Code § 8-27-3 states that a person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without privilege to do so, is liable for misappropriation if, among other things, 'That person's disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in that person by the other.' The weakened language in the modified contract makes it more difficult to establish a breach of confidence, thus creating a legal contradiction by undermining the intended protection of trade secrets under Alabama law.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall utilize no less than reasonable care in protecting Proprietary Information received from the disclosing party; such care shall be the same degree of care as though it were to protect its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall endeavor to use care in protecting Proprietary Information received from the disclosing party; such care may be similar to the degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall utilize no less than reasonable care" with "shall endeavor to use care" weakens the standard of care required. Replacing "shall be the same degree of care as though it were to protect" with "may be similar to the degree of care it uses to protect" further reduces the obligation, allowing for a potentially lower standard of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama Code § 8-27-1(2) defines "Misappropriation" as including the disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By weakening the duty of care, the modified clause makes it harder to demonstrate that the receiving party "knew or had reason to know" of their duty, hindering claims of misappropriation under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a specific standard of care for protecting proprietary information. The changed text weakens this standard by using terms like 'endeavor to use care' and 'may be similar,' introducing ambiguity and potentially allowing for a lower level of protection. The Law Explanation states this weakening makes it harder to demonstrate a breach of duty if misappropriation occurs under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Alabama Code § 8-27-1, establishes the short title for the Alabama Trade Secrets Act, which indirectly supports the claim that the weakened standard undermines the Act by potentially hindering the demonstration of 'knowledge' or 'reason to know' of a duty to maintain secrecy, a key element for misappropriation claims as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The change directly undermines the original intent to protect proprietary information to a specific standard.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return all Proprietary Information except that one copy may be retained by legal counsel of the receiving Party as evidence of what was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party should consider returning Proprietary Information; retaining a copy may be permissible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall promptly return" to "should consider returning" and "except that one copy may be retained" to "retaining a copy may be permissible" makes the return of proprietary information optional and ambiguous, leaving room for dispute about what constitutes a reasonable effort to return the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alabama Uniform Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. By making the return of proprietary information discretionary, the modified text increases the risk that trade secrets will be retained and potentially misused, undermining the Act's goal of preventing unauthorized use or disclosure.

A person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other for misappropriation of the trade secret if: (1) That person discovered the trade secret by improper means; (2) That person's disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in that person by the other; (3) That person learned the trade secret from a third person, and knew or should have known that (i) the information was a trade secret and (ii) that the trade secret had been appropriated under circumstances which violate the provisions of (1) or (2), above; or (4) That person learned the information and knew or should have known that it was a trade secret and that its disclosure was made to that person by mistake. perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return proprietary information, potentially conflicting with the Alabama Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original contract states the receiving party "shall promptly return all Proprietary Information," while the changed version says the receiving party "should consider returning Proprietary Information," making the return discretionary. The original text also allows for retaining one copy, whereas the changed text says "retaining a copy may be permissible", further softening the obligation. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022), "A person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other for misappropriation of the trade secret." By weakening the requirement to return proprietary information, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the intent of the law to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder may be subject to export control, and that compliance with all appropriate Government regulations such as the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (IT AR), the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), etc., may be necessary to obtain required approvals before disclosing Proprietary Information to foreign nationals, businesses or governments. The receiving Party shall obtain the written consent of the di sclosing Party prior to submitting any request for authority to export any such Proprietary Information. The receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the disclosing Party harmless from all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees , and all other expenses arising from failure of the receiving Party to comply with this clause or the ITAR or the EAR.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder might be subject to export control, and that awareness of relevant Government regulations such as the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (IT AR), the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), etc., may be beneficial before potentially disclosing Proprietary Information to foreign nationals, businesses or governments. The receiving Party will consider obtaining the consent of the disclosing Party prior to submitting any request related to exporting Proprietary Information. The receiving Party shall endeavor to indemnify and hold the disclosing Party harmless from claims arising from failure of the receiving Party to generally be aware of this clause or the ITAR or the EAR.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "may be subject" to "might be subject," "compliance with all appropriate Government regulations ... may be necessary" to "awareness of relevant Government regulations ... may be beneficial," "shall obtain the written consent" to "will consider obtaining the consent," and "shall indemnify and hold ... arising from failure ... to comply" to "shall endeavor to indemnify and hold ... arising from failure ... to generally be aware of" significantly weakens the obligations related to export control compliance. This makes compliance discretionary rather than mandatory and weakens the indemnity obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.1 outlines steps for determining export control requirements under the EAR. The modified text's weakening of obligations directly contradicts the mandatory compliance framework established by the EAR, potentially leading to unauthorized exports of controlled technology, since awareness and consideration are not equivalent to affirmative compliance.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates compliance with export control regulations, whereas the modified version weakens these obligations, making compliance discretionary. The original requires 'compliance with all appropriate Government regulations...may be necessary,' which is replaced with 'awareness of relevant Government regulations...may be beneficial.' Additionally, the original 'shall obtain the written consent' is changed to 'will consider obtaining the consent,' and the indemnity obligation is weakened. According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts the mandatory compliance framework of the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 CFR Part 732, outlines steps for using the EAR, implying a structured and mandatory process, which the modified text undermines by promoting mere 'awareness' and 'consideration,' rather than strict compliance. This significant weakening of obligations constitutes a legal contradiction, as the altered terms could lead to non-compliance with export control laws.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the Proprietary Information, and breach of this agreement by the receiving party would cause irreparable harm to th e disclosing party and that the disclosing party shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief in addition to all other remedies at law. The parties agree that, in the event of breach, or threatened breach of the terms of this agreement, the originating party may seek an injunction prohibiting such breach. Both parties hereby waiver any requirement to post bond for attempts to obtain such injunctive relief. Any such relief shall be in addition to and not in lieu of any appropriate relief in the way of m onetary damages. Each party shall pay its own attorney’s fees, costs, and expenses for any dispute under the agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that the Proprietary Information may have unique qualities, and a significant breach of this agreement could potentially cause harm to the disclosing party, so the disclosing party might be entitled to some form of relief beyond monetary damages. In the event of a potential breach, the originating party might consider seeking an injunction. Both parties might consider waiving the requirement to post bond. Any such relief should be in addition to other appropriate relief. Each party will generally be responsible for its own attorney’s fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "would cause irreparable harm" to "could potentially cause harm," "shall therefore be entitled to equitable relief" to "might be entitled to some form of relief," "may seek an injunction" to "might consider seeking an injunction," "hereby waive" to "might consider waiving," and "shall pay its own attorney's fees" to "will generally be responsible for its own attorney's fees" creates ambiguity regarding the certainty of harm, the availability of equitable relief, the likelihood of seeking an injunction, the waiver of bond, and the responsibility for attorney's fees. This makes enforcement uncertain and weakens the protections afforded to the disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alabama Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65(c) of the Alabama Rules of Civil Procedure typically requires the posting of a bond for preliminary injunctions or temporary restraining orders. While parties can contractually waive this requirement, the modified clause replaces a clear waiver with a statement that parties "might consider waiving", introducing ambiguity and potentially undermining the enforceability of the waiver. A judge may be less likely to enforce a discretionary waiver, potentially increasing the burden on the disclosing party to obtain injunctive relief.
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly stated obligations and rights (e.g., 'shall be entitled to equitable relief', 'hereby waiver'). The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens these previously clear statements by using terms like 'might consider' and 'potentially,' making the enforceability of these provisions uncertain. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this makes enforcement uncertain and weakens the protections afforded to the disclosing party. The Law Explanation adds that introducing ambiguity can undermine the enforceability of the waiver. Although no scraped snippets are available, the shift from definitive language to uncertain language creates a contradiction that undermines the original legal intent.
